coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of hydrothermal-assisted papaya peel-derived carbon quantum dots impregnated carboxymethyl cellulose and pectin crosslinked nanohydrogel for shelf-life enhancement of strawberry

coredata.dc:description: This study focuses on utilizing papaya peel to produce carbon quantum dots (PP-CQDs) via a hydrothermal method and the PP-CQDs are subsequently embedded into carboxymethyl cellulose and pectin nanohydrogel to enhance the shelf-life of strawberries. The synthesis of PP-CQDs was found to be optimal under conditions of 12 h and 200 °C, resulting in a quantum yield of 39.15 % and an average particle size of 4.16±0.07 nm. The PP-CQDs had intense blue luminescence and a peak absorbance at 262 nm when exposed to UV light. They also displayed unique fluorescence emission at 260 nm and excitation at 526 nm. Particle size was not significant for both control (176.4 ± 0.18 nm) and PP-CQDs embedded nanohydrogel (175.8 ± 0.09 nm). The PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite demonstrated high cell viability (95.12±0.78 to 98.43±0.96 %) as well as excellent antibacterial efficacy against pathogenic Staphylococcus aureus bacteria during time-kill and anti-quorum activity. Both types of coated strawberries exhibited non-significant differences in weight loss (16.85±0.18 and 16.34±0.39 %) as compared to uncoated strawberries (20.87±0.42 %) during storage. The firmness of both coated strawberries was found to be significantly higher as compared to uncoated samples, while strawberries coated with composite nanohydrogel showed enhanced microbial stability. Overall, PP-CQDs embedded nanohydrogel could be a promising coating material to enhance the shelf-life of highly perishable fruits.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Visual confirmation of PP-CQDs under daylight and UV-light at different temperature combinations (A) PP-CQDs at 100 °C under daylight, (B) PP-CQDs at 150 °C under daylight, (C) PP-CQDs at 200 °C under daylight, (D) PP-CQDs at 100 °C under UV- light, (E) PP-CQDs at 150 °C under UV- light, (F) PP-CQDs at 200 °C under UV- light.

Characterization of PP-CQDs (papaya peel-derived Carbon quantum dots) synthesized at different temperatures of PP-CQDs at 100 °C, PP-CQDs at 150 °C, and PP-CQDs at 200 °C: (A) UV–visible spectrum, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra, (C) Fluorescence stability, (D) Average particle size, (E) Zeta potential, (F) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, (G) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy, (H) Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy mapping, (I) Differential scanning calorimetry, (J) Atomic Force Microscopy, (K) X-ray diffraction, (L) 13C NMR (Carbon-13 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance) Spectroscopy, and (M) 1H NMR (Proton-Nuclear Magnetic Resonance) Spectroscopy.

(A) and (B) Droplet size and zeta potential of control hydrogel (CH) and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite (PP-CQDs NH), (C) Cytotoxicity of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite (D) Zone of inhibition of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite against Gram positive and Gram negative bacteria (E) MIC of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite, (F) MBC of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite.

Physico-chemical characteristics of uncoated (US), control hydrogel coated (CHS), and PP-CQDs nano hydrogel coated strawberries (PP-CQDs NHS) (A) Water loss, (B) pH, (C) TSS, (D) Firmness and (E) Actual photos illustration of PP-CQDs NHS coated for strawberry preservation. The error bar represents the standard deviation from the mean of three independent replications (n = 3).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study focuses on utilizing papaya peel to produce carbon quantum dots (PP-CQDs) via a hydrothermal method and the PP-CQDs are subsequently embedded into carboxymethyl cellulose and pectin nanohydrogel to enhance the shelf-life of strawberries. The synthesis of PP-CQDs was found to be optimal under conditions of 12 h and 200 °C, resulting in a quantum yield of 39.15 % and an average particle size of 4.16±0.07 nm. The PP-CQDs had intense blue luminescence and a peak absorbance at 262 nm when exposed to UV light. They also displayed unique fluorescence emission at 260 nm and excitation at 526 nm. Particle size was not significant for both control (176.4 ± 0.18 nm) and PP-CQDs embedded nanohydrogel (175.8 ± 0.09 nm). The PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite demonstrated high cell viability (95.12±0.78 to 98.43±0.96 %) as well as excellent antibacterial efficacy against pathogenic Staphylococcus aureus bacteria during time-kill and anti-quorum activity. Both types of coated strawberries exhibited non-significant differences in weight loss (16.85±0.18 and 16.34±0.39 %) as compared to uncoated strawberries (20.87±0.42 %) during storage. The firmness of both coated strawberries was found to be significantly higher as compared to uncoated samples, while strawberries coated with composite nanohydrogel showed enhanced microbial stability. Overall, PP-CQDs embedded nanohydrogel could be a promising coating material to enhance the shelf-life of highly perishable fruits.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, the improper disposal of papaya peels has emerged as a significant environmental concern, particularly in developing countries, posing a major challenge for waste management systems [1]. This issue provides an opportunity for waste valorization through the conversion of papaya peels into carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a high-value nanomaterial [2]. This transformation not only aligns with the principles of green chemistry by reducing waste but also promotes sustainability by repurposing bio-based materials [3]. In addition to papaya peels, a variety of other food waste sources can be utilized for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [4]. Orange peels, rich in pectin and flavonoids, offer a promising raw material for CQDs with applications in sensing and imaging. Banana peels, high in carbohydrates and potassium, serve as a cost-effective source for CQDs, beneficial for bioimaging and drug delivery [5]. Apple pomace, a byproduct from apple juice production, contains polyphenols and cellulose, making it suitable for CQD synthesis. Coffee grounds, which include caffeine and other organic compounds, can be used for CQD production with potential applications in environmental monitoring and catalysis [6]. Additionally, tomato waste, abundant in lycopene and other organic substances, provides another valuable source for CQDs, with potential uses in biosensing and photothermal therapy. However, this research specifically highlights the unique advantages of papaya peels due to their high carbon content and sustainability, demonstrating their superior efficacy and environmental benefits in CQD synthesis compared to other sources [7].

The use of papaya peels as a carbon source for carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesis entails hydrothermal carbonization, a technique preferred for its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendliness [8]. This approach produces small, fluorescent nanoparticles by converting the carbon-rich peels, with potential applications in various fields [9]. This strategy addresses environmental problems related to papaya peel disposal while contributing to nanotechnology by offering a sustainable source of valuable materials, thereby supporting circular economy principles and promoting sustainable practices in waste management and material production. [10]. CQDs can be synthesized through a top-down or bottom-up approach, with the hydrothermal method being particularly notable for producing highly crystalline and uniformly sized CQDs with excellent photoluminescence properties [11]. Hydrothermal carbonization is a subset of bottom-up techniques that employ high temperature and pressure conditions in an aqueous solution to convert organic precursors [12]. These nanometer-sized carbon-based materials exhibit unique optical and electronic properties, including excellent photoluminescence, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and robust chemical stability, making them suitable for different applications in bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, and optoelectronics [13]. However, traditional CD produce methods are frequently laborious and environmentally harmful. The hydrothermal approach reduces these challenges while providing a more environmentally friendly solution. The increasing incidence of infectious diseases caused by microorganisms presents a significant challenge to global public health [14]. Studies have shown that CQDs exhibit antimicrobial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, primarily through electrostatic interactions and disruption of microbial membranes [15]. These interactions facilitate the adherence of CQDs to bacterial surfaces, where they can induce oxidative stress, generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), and disrupt membrane integrity [16]. This mechanism underscores the potential of CQDs as effective antimicrobial agents.

In addition to biomedical applications, this study explores the development of a hydrogel using carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and pectin, two polysaccharides renowned for their gel-forming abilities and biocompatibility [17]. The hydrogel's structural integrity, mechanical strength, and stability are all improved as a result of the crosslinking of these polymers, which makes the hydrogel an ideal matrix for embedding CQD systems. [18]. The cross-linking process involves forming covalent bonds between CMC and pectin molecules, resulting in a robust network that improves the hydrogel's barrier properties and durability [19].

The hydrogel serves as a matrix for embedding CQDs, facilitating the formation of a nanocomposite material tailored for coating applications [20]. The synergistic effect of CMC and pectin in the hydrogel matrix, enhanced by crosslinking, provides a robust and effective barrier [21]. This hydrogel composite was specifically applied to strawberries to test its efficacy in prolonging shelf life and maintaining fruit quality during storage.

The application of the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite on strawberries demonstrated notable improvements in the physicochemical properties of the fruits, effectively reducing weight loss and enhancing microbial stability, thereby prolonging the shelf life of highly perishable fruits [22]. The cross-linked structure of the hydrogel plays a crucial role in maintaining these benefits by ensuring the mechanical strength and stability of the coating, thus supporting its effectiveness throughout the storage period [23].

In this work, papaya peel waste was used as a raw material to synthesize CQDs using a hydrothermal method without the addition of passivation or additives which is cost-effective and environmentally friendly. In conclusion, the development of carbon quantum dots from agricultural waste such as papaya peels presents a dual opportunity to address environmental issues and create PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite as a coating agent on the strawberry surface for food preservation applications. The droplet size, cytotoxicity, and antimicrobial activity of prepared hydrogels were measured. The effect and mechanism of crosslinked nanohydrogels on delaying strawberry decay were investigated by analyzing its physiochemical properties including water loss, pH, TSS, and firmness. Furthermore, the microbial analysis of strawberries was tested to evaluate the shelf-life of the fruit. The integration of these PP-CQDs into a biocompatible hydrogel for fruit coating applications illustrates the potential of interdisciplinary strategies in addressing real-world challenges, advancing a circular economy, and enhancing the sustainability of the agricultural and materials science sectors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Papaya peels as raw material were collected from Punjab Agro Industries Corporation Limited (Chandigarh, India). Fresh strawberries were harvested from the cultivation area of Lovely Professional University (Jalandhar, India) and thoroughly rinsed multiple times with deionized water. The experiments required analytical grade ethanol and sodium hydroxide of Sigma-Aldrich, USA. In addition, the dialysis membrane and syringe filters of 3KDa and 0.45 μm were purchased from Merck Millipore and Cole-Parmer, India, respectively. To evaluate the antimicrobial activity, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginsonsa), Klebsiella pneumoniae (K. pneumoniae), Escherichia coli (E. coli), and Salmonella typhi (S. typhi) strains were procured from IMTECH institute (Chandigarh, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods
Synthesis of papaya peel-derived carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal method was employed for the synthesis of PP-CQDs as the method described by Shahraki et al., [24]. Afterward, 1 g of the sample was dissolved in 100 mL of milli-Q water (solvent) and then transferred into a Teflon-lined steel reactor.Then, the reactor-containing solution was placed in an oven heated at different temperature combinations of 100, 150, and 200 °C for 10 h for optimization of the final sample. The resulting product was centrifuged at 10,000 ×g for 20 min to achieve a homogeneous solution with complete dispersion. After incubation, the supernatant was filtered using a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.45 μm. The filtered liquid was then placed in a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 3KDa and dialyzed for 20 min to achieve a clear solution. Finally, the dialysate was stored in refrigeration at 4 °C. The final sample was selected based on color under day light and UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of the PP-CQDs derived from papaya peel was performed using multiple spectroscopic techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of yellow color of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A white light source was used to verify the synthesis of PP-CQDs. The fluorescence of the PP-CQDs exhibited a clearly noticeable yellow color when illuminated. In order to improve the ability to see colors, PP-CQD samples were carefully transferred into a 3.5 mL quartz cuvette. After that, the color of the solution was examined, and photos recorded to document the distinct yellow color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of blue color of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the successful synthesis of PP-CQDs, we utilized a UV-transilluminator. The intended blue coloration of PP-CQDs became apparent as the CQDs exhibited distinctive blue fluorescence under UV light. A dispersed samples of PP-CQDs were carefully transferred into a 3.5 mL quartz cuvette, which was then positioned in the UV-transilluminator in a dark room. Subsequently, the UV-transilluminator was activated, and photos were taken to capture the desired blue fluorescence. This approach ensures a thorough confirmation of the successful synthesis of PP-CQDs under UV illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis spectroscopy of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible absorption spectra of the PP-CQDs were assessed by recording them using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2700i, Kyoto, Japan), following the method outlined by Sun et al., [25]. The instrument was operated in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The reference material utilized was Milli-Q water, which is renowned for its 100 % reflectance. To measure the absorbance, 1 mL of the produced PP-CQDs samples were mixed with 2 mL of Milli-Q water to ensure even distribution. The spectrum was conducted using the UV-probe software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopy of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of the PP-CQD samples were collected using an F-2500 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Spectrum FL version 1.0.0, Germany) by following the method detailed by Pooja et al., [26]. The following parameters were used as λex = 260 nm with a scan wavelength of 800 to 350 nm at a scanning speed of 10 nm min−1 for the collection of fluorescence results. In addition, both the emission and excitation slit width were set at 5 and 2 nm, respectively. The resultant peak was observed on the graph by using Spectrum FLR version 1.1 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and Zeta potential of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The average particle size and total surface charge distribution of PP-CQDs were evaluated using a Malvern Zetasizer (Nano ZS90, Germany), as the method outlined by Lim, [27]. The 1 mL PP-CQDs were mixed with Milli-Q water and then homogenized for 20 min via probe sonicator (Labman Scientific Instruments Ltd., Chennai, India).The homogenized solution was then transferred to a 4 mL quartz cuvette and analyzed.. Zetasizer version 7.02 software was used for plotting the graphs with each measurement conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of the PP-CQDs were obtained using a Shimadzu IRTracer from Kyoto, Japan, for surface functional group analysis as described by method of Velmurugan et al., [28]. The sample (25 μL) was placed onto detector, and the transmittance mode's range was adjusted to 400–4000 cm-1 with a resolution of 0.5 cm-1. The spectrum was then recorded in terms of wavenumber and transmittance.Each final spectrum represented the average of 100 scans by using Spectrum 10 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal morphological behavior and elemental analysis of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size, morphology, and elemental analysis of PP-CQDs were examined using HR-TEM and EDS (Thermo Scientific TalosTM F200X G2, Massachusetts, U·S) according to the method described by Wang et al., [29]. The instruments were operated at an acceleration voltage of 20 kV of HR-TEM and 10 kV of EDS. The samples were prepared to make it conductive by spreading 10 μL of the liquid sample (PP-CQDs) on a copper grid and allowing it to dry for 2 min at a voltage of 30 mA. Subsequently, the Thermo ScientificTM Velox program was employed to acquire the images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal analysis was conducted using a TA Instruments Q20 DSC (Japan) under a nitrogen environment with a specific flow rate of 50 mL/min according to the method proposed by Jhonsi and Asha, [30]. A 20 μl sample of PP-CQDs was heated at a rate of 10 °C/min from 25 °C to 350 °C to examine the phase change behavior. The key parameters analyzed the melting temperature, glass transition temperature, and thermal stability by onset and endset temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomic force microscopy (AFM) of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the synthesized PP-CQDs was examined using AFM (Brisk Model instrument, Research Ara Co., Iran) following the protocol outlined by Gao et al., [31]. A diluted dispersion of PP-CQDs in deionized water was applied onto a silicon wafer that had been exposed to UV radiation. After that, the wafer was left to desiccate at a room temperature (28 °C). The scanning rate was maintained between 0.5 and 1 line per second, for optimal image resolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray diffraction (XRD) of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to assess the crystallinity and phase purity of the synthesized PP-CQDs by following the method of Aouadi et al., [32]. The analysis was carried out using a “Rigaku Miniflex 600” X-ray diffractometer. The measurements were performed over a 2θ range of 10 to 80°, utilizing Cu-Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å. The diffractometer was operated at a voltage of 45 kV and a current of 40 mA to ensure accurate and reliable data collection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 13C NMR and 1H NMR analysis of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the analysis of the synthesized PP-CQDs, both proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) and carbon nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR) spectra were recorded using an Avance III HD 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, USA) by following the procedure outlined by Garg et al., [33]. Deuterium oxide (D₂O) was used as the solvent for both types of NMR analysis to dissolve the samples effectively. The spectrometer was operated at a frequency of 100 MHz. The number of scans was carefully selected to improve the signal-to-noise ratio, ensuring clear identification of the carbon environments. Both analyses were performed under conditions that provided detailed insight into the molecular structure of the PP-CQDs, with particular attention to the precision of chemical shift assignments and the clarity of spectral peaks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quantum yield of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield of the PP-CQDs was determined by V. B. Kumar et al., [34], the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ =
Φr × I / Ir × Ar / A × η / ηr

Here, Φ represents the comparative quantum yield of the CQDs, referenced against quinine sulfate, which has a concentration of 0.1 mg/L in 0.1 M H2SO4 and is used as the standard (Φr) at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. “I” is the integrated emission intensity from the emission spectrum at 360 nm excitation. “A” denotes the UV–visible absorbance at 360 nm, maintained below 0.05 in a 10 mm quartz cuvette to prevent re-absorption effects. The solvent's refractive index (η) was measured to be 1.33.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence decay time measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence decay time of PP-CQDs was measured using time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy as a method described by Dong et al., [35]. The experiment was conducted with a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system. The PP-CQDs were prepared in milli-Q water and placed in a sample holder within the spectrometer. A pulsed laser (PicoQuant LDH-P-C-470) with an excitation wavelength of 470 nm was used to excite the sample. The emitted fluorescence was detected by a photomultiplier tube (Hamamatsu R928) connected to a TCSPC module (PicoQuant HydraHarp 400). The TCSPC module recorded the time delay between the excitation pulse and the arrival of emitted photons. The resultant decay was analyzed using the PicoQuant SymPhoTime 64 software, which fitted the data to a multi-exponential decay model. This analysis provided the fluorescence decay times, allowing for the determination of the photophysical properties of the PP-CQDs. The measurements were repeated at least three times to ensure reproducibility and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of the control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: The control and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite were prepared with food-grade carboxymethyl cellulose(CMC) and pectin. Briefly, 4 g CMC and 2 g pectin were slowly added to 250 mL of deionized water with continuous stirring at 800 rpm on a magnetic stirrer. After complete dissolution, the solution was heated at 85 °C for 35 min for gelatinization. After that, the prepared PP-CQDs were transferred to the hydrogel solution and mixed until they were evenly distributed in order to produce the composite hydrogel. In addition, control hydrogel was formulated without the addition of prepared PP-CQDs. After that, both the hydrogels were stored under aseptical conditions at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling and release properties of control and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para:
Swelling studies were conducted by first recording the dry weight (dw) of each hydrogel sample as a method mentioned by Zhang et al., [36]. The samples were then immersed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.4 and incubated at 37 °C. At time intervals (e.g., 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24, and 48 h), the hydrogel samples were removed, gently blotted to remove excess surface water, and weighed to obtain the swollen weight (sw). The swelling ratio (SR) was calculated using the Eq. (2)
(2)
SR =
s w − dw
dw

For the release studies, a model drug (e.g., Rhodamine B) was loaded into the hydrogels by incorporating it into the hydrogel precursor solution before cross-linking. In the case of the PPCQD-embedded hydrogel, both PP-CQDs and the model drug were incorporated into the hydrogel matrix. Drug-loaded hydrogel samples were immersed in PBS at pH 7.4 and incubated at 37 °C. At time intervals, aliquots of the release medium were collected and replaced with fresh PBS. The concentration of the released drug in the collected aliquots was measured to determine the release mechanism and the influence of PP-CQDs on the release profile.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
Droplet size and zeta potential analysis of control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: The droplet particle size and zeta potential of the control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite were analyzed by diluting with Milli-Q water to achieve the optimal concentration for measurement by using particle size analyzer. The measurements were performed with a detector angle of 90° at 25 °C. The hydrogels were homogenized using an ultrasonicator for 20 min before the measurements were conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the cytotoxicity of PP-CQDs, the control hydrogel, and the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite, the MTT test is employed. The first step involves introducing HeLa cells into a 96-well plate and enabling them to adhere and achieve the necessary level of cell density. The cells with varying concentrations of the test substance were incubated for 24 h. After treatment, the 0.5 mg/mL MTT reagent (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) was added to each well and incubated for 3–4 h. The MTT is reduced by metabolically active cells to form insoluble formazan crystals, indicating cell viability. The culture medium was removed carefully and dissolved into 100 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). The absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (Thermofisher Scientific, USA). The cell viability was calculated using the specified Eq. (3).
(3)
Cell viability
%
=
AB
sample − AB
negative control
AB
positive control − AB
negative control
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial activity of PP-CQDs, the control hydrogel, and the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite was determined against five microorganisms including S. aureus, K. pneumoniae, E. coli, S. typhi, and P. aeruginosa. The agar well diffusion method was performed according to the procedure described by Pant et al. [37]. Briefly, the wells were prepared using a sterile cork borer in Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) plates, and various Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial suspensions at a concentration of 1 × 108 CFU/mL were evenly distributed on the agar surface with a sterile cotton swab. Chloramphenicol served as a positive control and DMSO as a negative control, alongside all the prepared samples, which were then placed into the wells. The zones of inhibition (ZOI) around each well were measured to determine the antibacterial activity of each sample against the different bacterial strains after a 24-hour incubation at 37 °C. Furthermore, the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) were assessed using the broth microdilution method as described by Koutamehr et al., [38]. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, with the MIC identified as the lowest concentration of PP-CQDs that visibly inhibited bacterial growth. The MBC was established by subculturing the contents from the MIC wells onto MHA plates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofilm activity of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para:
The antibiofilm activity of PP-CQDs, the control hydrogel, and the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite was detected using the ELISA method [39]. Initially, 50 μL of the bacterial cell suspension of microorganisms such as E. coli, S. typhi, P. aeruginosa, and K. pneumoniae, S. aureus was added to a 96-well plate to allow biofilm formation and incubating under appropriate conditions. After the biofilm was established, the wells with 100 μL of each prepared sample was incubated at 37 °C for a duration of 72 h to assess its antibiofilm effects. Following treatment, the wells to remove non-adherent substances rinse with 0.9 % NaCl was gently washed, and then dried at 60 °C for 15 min. The crystal violet dye was added to each well, and the plates were incubated at 27 °C for 15 min. After that, the color change was measured using a microplate reader at 595 nm wavelength. The following Eq. (4) was employed to ascertain the percent inhibition of biofilm formation.
(4)
Inhibition
%
= 1 −
The absorbance of test sample Absorbance of control
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Killing kinetics according to incubation time of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite against microbial populations was investigated according to their impact on bacterial growth over time by following the method of Jun Won and Kim, [40]. Briefly, the bacterial suspensions were cultured in Mueller Hinton broth and were monitored for changes in population size through viable cell counts. The bacterial growth was assessed at 0, 24, and 48 h. The effectiveness of PP-CQDs, the control hydrogel, and the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite in decreasing microbial populations was determined by measuring the logarithm of colony-forming units per milliliter (log CFU/mL) at different time intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation and coating of strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare fresh strawberries for hydrogel coating, firstly ripe and undamaged strawberries were selected and thoroughly washed with distilled water to remove any dirt and contaminants. The strawberries were allowed to air dry. The hydrogel solutions were prepared and each strawberry was dipped into the hydrogel solution (control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite), ensuring an even coating over the entire surface for 10 min. Then the coated strawberries were placed on a drying rack and allowed to dry at 27 °C for 30 min, and subsequently stored at refrigerated temperatures (4–7 °C) for 10 days for further use or analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of strawberries coated with formulated hydrogel
Weight loss percentage and pH of strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: The weight loss analysis of both treated and non-treated strawberries with formulated hydrogel followed the protocol outlined by K. Chen et al., [41] with minor modifications. Specifically, the 5 g of each sample was placed on a glass petri plate and stored under a low temperature of 4–7 °C. After that, the loss in weight was recorded at several intervals such as specifically on days 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 during storage. In addition, the acidity of the strawberry samples was assessed following the procedure by R. Kumar et al., [42]. The pH of strawberry samples was determined using a digital pH meter (Labtronics Led Auto Deluxe pH meter, LT-10, Delhi, India). The samples of 15 g were blended with 40 mL of distilled water, and then the mixture was analyzed to determine the pH values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total soluble solids (TSS) of strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of total soluble solids of both treated and untreated strawberry samples was conducted according to Sawsan et al., [43]. In the process, the 40 g of strawberries were mixed with 80 mL of distilled water and then homogenized using a handheld laboratory mixer to produce a mixture. The mixture was passed through Whatman filter paper number 1. Subsequently, it was centrifuged at 6500 ×g for 20 min to clarify the sample The TSS content of the samples was measured using A digital refractometer (by Optima Digital ABBE Refractometer, OIAR-1317, Vadodara, Gujarat, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Firmness analysis of strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: The firmness of both treated and untreated strawberry samples was assessed using a method proposed by Huang et al., [44]. A texture analyzer (Shimadzu EZ-Test texture analyzer, Tamil Nadu, India) was utilized to measure the firmness of these samples. For this analysis, the probe with a 9 mm diameter and depth of 15 mm with a constant speed of 15 mm/s penetrated a strawberry sample of 24 mm thickness to exert a standardized force. Six samples of strawberries for each group were randomly selected for testing on the 0th, 2nd, 4th, 6th, 8th, and 10th day of storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial analysis of strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: Microbiological evaluations were performed as described by Kifle et al., [45] to assess the presence of total bacteria, coliforms, yeast & mold, in strawberry samples. These assessments were conducted on 25 g of uncoated strawberries, strawberries coated with the control hydrogel, and the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite after homogenization with a sterile blender to obtain a uniform sample. After homogenizing, the samples were diluted to facilitate the counting of microorganisms. The 25 g of strawberries were homogenized in 225 mL of sterile buffer, this yields a 1:10 dilution. Measurements were taken in triplicate at 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10-day intervals. Yeast and mold count plates were incubated at 20 °C for 10 days. After incubation, colonies were enumerated with a digital colony counter (Labtronics Model no. LT-37, Panchkula, India), and the results were expressed as colony-forming units per gram (log CFU/g) of strawberry samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensory analysis of strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: A sensory evaluation was conducted with male and female semi-trained panelists (n = 35) aged between 25 and 45 years. They assessed uncoated strawberries, strawberries coated with the control hydrogel, and the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite. The evaluations were carried out in a sensory laboratory, featuring individual booths following the International Organization for Standardization guidelines (EC/LPU/23/215). To prevent bias, each strawberry sample was placed on a dish labeled with a random, anonymous code. The panelists rated the samples on attributes such as texture, flavor, appearance, and overall acceptability using a 9-point hedonic scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The study presented the data as mean with standard deviations. To analyze the differences between the average means of the experimental samples, an analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed. The critical difference (CD) was used to statistically compare the means. All calculations were carried out using the “data analysis” tool in Microsoft Excel 2016. This approach ensured statistical results and facilitated the clear presentation of the data [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs can be synthesized from various organic and inorganic carbon-rich materials. This study focused on the synthesis and analysis of PP-CQDs and the development of a hydrogel derived from easily accessible, economical, and environmentally friendly fruit waste, namely papaya waste. These materials were then explored for their potential uses in various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization studies of PP-CQDs
Determination of the yellow color of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PP-CQDs samples at different time and temperature combinations of 100, 150, and 200 °C were selected for the determination of yellow color and results are represented in Figs. 1A, B, and C. All CQDs are known for their excellent optical properties, which include strong luminescence. The color of the luminescence is dependent on several factors, including the size of the quantum dots, the surface states, and the functional groups attached to their surface. To confirm the synthesis of PP-CQDs the desired yellow color was observed as the CQDs show yellow fluorescence under a light source. Moreover, the PP-CQDs synthesized at 100 and 150 °C temperatures showed significantly (p < 0.05) lighter yellow color than that of 200 °C. However, the PP-CQDs synthesized at 200 °C for 10 h were screened out for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the blue color of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PP-CQDs samples at different time and temperature combinations of 100, 150, and 200 °C were selected for the determination of blue color and results are indicated in Fig. 1D, E, and F. This luminescence can be visible under specific conditions, such as when exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light. The blue color exhibited by CQDs under UV light is primarily attributed to their size-dependent optical properties, specifically their fluorescence emission. In the case of blue fluorescence, it typically arises from quantum confinement effects within the CQDs. Quantum confinement occurs when the size of the CQDs is comparable to or smaller than the wavelength of the emitted light. As a result, the energy levels of electrons within the CQDs become quantized, leading to discrete energy transitions and the emission of light with shorter wavelengths, such as blue. The PP-CQDs synthesized at 100 and 150 °C temperatures showed a significantly (p < 0.05) lighter blue color than that of 200 °C. The desired blue color was observed in PP-CQDs synthesized at 200 °C for 10 h and used for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A provided a UV–visible spectroscopy plot for carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from papaya peel waste at different temperature combinations of 100, 150, and 200 °C for 10 h. The x-axis represents the wavelength in nanometers (nm), typically ranging from 200 to 800 nm, and the y-axis represents the absorbance. The spectra of the unselected sample, which was synthesized under low-temperature conditions of 100 and 150 °C, reveal a broad and weak absorbance band in the UV region, specifically around 250–280 nm. This weak absorbance suggests a lower concentration of conjugated π-electron systems typically associated with the CQDs. The broadness of the band indicates a lack of uniformity in the size and surface states of the CQDs, a characteristic often seen in materials synthesized under suboptimal conditions. In comparison, the selected sample, synthesized under 200 °C for 10 h, exhibited a stronger and sharper absorbance peak in the UV region, typically around 262 nm. This stronger absorbance indicates a higher concentration of π-conjugated systems and better-defined CQD surface states. The sharper and more intense absorbance peaks in the selected sample suggest that these CQDs have better-defined optical properties, making them more suitable for applications. This absorption peak is an important indicator of the electronic properties and structural features of the CQDs derived from papaya peel waste. These results are similar to those reported by Nizam et al., [47], where the UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs derived from rubber seed shells showed a broad peak at 340 nm, and another peak at 250 nm, originating from the n-π* transition of the CO or COOH functional group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of fluorescence spectroscopy and stability of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectrum graph of PP-CQDs synthesized at different temperatures of 100, 150, and 200 °C illustrates the emission properties of CQDs with significant differences in both peak wavelength and intensity across the samples in Fig. 2B. The fluorescence spectroscopy results of synthesized PP-CQDs at lower temperatures of 100 and 150 °C would likely indicate a lower emission intensity and potentially a red-shifted peak than the sample synthesized at 200 °C. This behavior suggests that the CQDs synthesized at lower temperatures might have larger particle sizes and less efficient surface passivation, which could result in weaker photoluminescent properties. The red shift in emission could be due to the presence of more defects or less uniform surface functionalization, affecting the electronic structure and reducing the quantum confinement effects. In contrast, the sample synthesized at 200 °C, which exhibited the smallest particle size, demonstrates a higher fluorescence intensity and a blue-shifted peak, indicative of more efficient quantum confinement and well-passivated surface states. This highlights the influence of synthesis temperature on the optical properties of PP-CQDs, where higher temperatures facilitate the formation of smaller, more optically active nanoparticles. The fluorescence mechanism involves electron-hole recombination, where the quantum confinement leads to discrete energy levels, and the surface states further modulate the emission properties by trapping and releasing photo-generated carriers. Han et al., [48] found that waste fruit peel-derived CQDs possess unique and tunable fluorescence properties. The CQDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence under UV light, with an excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior.

The stability analysis of the selected PP-CQDs, as shown in the fluorescence spectroscopy graph of Fig. 2C, indicates that the fluorescence intensity remains consistent over varying durations of 40, 80, and 120 min. The emission peak remains centered around 530 nm across all time intervals, with only slight variations in intensity, which suggests that the photoluminescent properties of the CQDs are stable over time. This peak wavelength and intensity consistency demonstrate that the PP-CQDs maintain their structural integrity and optical properties even after extended periods, indicating good stability under the given conditions. Such stability is crucial for potential applications in bioimaging, sensing, and other fields where long-term fluorescence consistency is required. Our results are similar to the findings of Tian et al., [49], who observed a wavelength-dependent shift in excitation and photoluminescence (PL) peaks. In this study, when excitation wavelength was increased from 320 to 480 nm then the PL peak shifted to 430 to 560 nm. Notably, the CQDs derived from the hydrogenated TiO2 nanobelt heterostructures demonstrated peak PL intensity under an excitation wavelength of 450 nm, with a maximum emission at 510 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size, zeta potential, and polydispersity index of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size analysis was done to determine the particle size distribution of synthesized PP-CQDs at 100, 150, and 200 °C as shown in Figs. 2D, with the results of zeta potential shown in Figs. 2E, respectively. The unselected CQDs synthesized at 100 °C show a broad distribution with a peak around 19.01 ±0.09 nm and noticeable aggregation, indicating poor size control with zeta potential of −12 Mv. At 150 °C, the size distribution becomes slightly narrower, with a more pronounced peak around 16.13 ± 0.07 nm, but some aggregation persists with −18 mV zeta potential. In contrast, the selected CQDs synthesized at 200 °C exhibit a sharp, well-defined peak around 4.16 ± 0.07 nm, with minimal aggregation, reflecting a more uniform particle size distribution with −21 mV zeta potential. This suggests that higher synthesis temperatures provide better control over the nucleation and growth processes, resulting in more consistent and homogeneous CQDs, which are advantageous for applications requiring precise particle sizes, such as in bioimaging or sensing. Moreover, the low surface charge promotes a more uniform particle size distribution, resulting in relatively better stability and reduced aggregation. These findings suggest that the surface passivation of carbon quantum dots involves the functionalization of their surface with various chemical groups. This process can influence the size and properties of the dots. To evaluate the stability and uniformity of the PP-CQDs, the Polydispersity Index (PDI) was calculated. Prior studies have indicated that a PDI value nearing 0 suggests the presence of monodisperse particles, whereas polydisperse particles typically exhibit PDI values approaching 1. The 0.201 PDI value of selected PP-CQDs observed indicates that the sample exhibits the uniform size distribution of particles in suspension with some degree of polydispersity. A lower PDI suggests a more consistent size and shape of CQDs, leading to better quantum confinement effects. In smaller-sized CQDs, quantum confinement becomes more pronounced, influencing their electronic structure and, consequently, their optical properties, including photoluminescence. Rojas-Valencia et al., [50] demonstrated that higher synthesis temperatures result in smaller CQD size distributions for Hibiscus Sabdariffa-derived particles. These results are obtained at 400 °C, followed by 300 °C and 200 °C, suggesting a direct correlation between carbonization degree and particle size. Notably, the highest temperatures and longer exposure time gave the smallest CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR analysis of PP-CQDs synthesized at different temperatures of 100, 150, and 200 °C reveal various peaks corresponding to specific functional groups on the surface of the CQDs as depicted in Fig. 2F. As observed, the sample synthesized at 200 °C shows the most pronounced peaks, indicating a higher concentration of surface functional groups. The broad peak around 3300 cm−1, which corresponds to OH stretching vibrations, is most intense in the sample synthesized at 200 °C, signifying a greater presence of hydroxyl groups. This suggests that higher synthesis temperatures promote the formation of these hydrophilic groups, enhancing the solubility and reactivity of the CQDs. The OH peak is less intense in the 150 °C sample and even weaker in the 100 °C sample, indicating a reduced presence of hydroxyl groups as the synthesis temperature decreases. The CH stretching vibrations around 2100–2200 cm−1, indicative of aliphatic -CH2 and -CH3 groups, are also more pronounced in the 200 °C sample. This suggests that higher temperatures favor the formation of aliphatic hydrocarbons on the CQD surface, contributing to better surface passivation. The intensity of these peaks diminishes in the 150 °C and 100 °C samples, reflecting a decrease in surface passivation and potentially impacting the photoluminescent properties of the CQDs. The CO stretching vibrations around 1636 cm−1, associated with carbonyl groups such as ketones, aldehydes, or carboxylic acids, are again most prominent in the 200 °C sample. This peak is less distinct in the samples synthesized at lower temperatures, indicating a reduction in the carbonyl group content, which could lead to decreased photoluminescence efficiency due to fewer surface passivation sites. Finally, the peaks around 500–600 cm−1, corresponding to CO stretching vibrations, are also strongest in the 200 °C sample, suggesting a higher presence of alcohol, ether, or ester groups. These peaks are less intense in the samples synthesized at 150 °C and 100 °C, indicating lower chemical functionality and hydrophilicity at these lower temperatures. The mechanism behind their photoluminescent properties involves these surface functional groups, which play a crucial role in passivating surface defects and enhancing the emission efficiency of the CQDs by trapping and recombining charge carriers. These identified functional groups elucidate the surface chemistry of the PP-CQDs, highlighting their potential for applications in sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery. This observation is consistent with prior research conducted by Jamaludin et al., [51], who reported similar findings regarding the presence of oxygen-rich groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl, as well as other functionalities in CQDs derived from empty fruit bunch biochar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM and EDS of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2G display the HR-TEM image of the PP-CQDs, which were synthesized using papaya peel in Milli-Q water as the solvent at different temperature combinations of 100, 150, and 200 °C, respectively. In the TEM image at the lower temperature, the PP-CQDs exhibit an average particle size of approximately 1.16 ± 0.09 nm, with particles showing a tendency to aggregate. As the synthesis temperature increases, the particle size decreases, as seen in the TEM image of the 150 °C temperature sample, where the average particle size is reduced to 1.07 ± 0.06 nm. At the highest synthesis temperature, the TEM image reveals that the PP-CQDs have further reduced in size to an average of 0.74 ± 0.04 nm, with the particles appearing highly uniform and well-dispersed, indicating optimal synthesis conditions. In Fig. 2H, the EDS spectrum of the PP-CQDs provides insight into the elemental composition of the samples. The corresponding EDS spectrum of PP-CQDs synthesized at 100 °C reveals that these CQDs are primarily composed of carbon and oxygen, with a relatively high oxygen content (76.38 wt%), suggesting the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, likely due to incomplete carbonization at the lower synthesis temperature. The EDS spectrum for the sample synthesized at 150 °C shows a higher proportion of carbon (28.72 wt%) relative to oxygen (71.28 wt%), suggesting that the increase in temperature enhances the carbonization process, reducing the number of oxygen-containing functional groups. The spectrum of selected PP-CQDs confirms a significant increase in carbon content (49.79 wt%) and a corresponding decrease in oxygen content (50.21 wt%), reflecting more complete carbonization. Notably, this method represents the first reported approach for synthesizing CQDs from papaya peel, resulting in a high carbon purity of 49.79 wt%. This elemental composition supports the mechanism of improved optical properties through effective surface passivation and reduced defect states. These observations are consistent with the findings of Yang et al., [52], who reported a size distribution below 5 nm through TEM analysis and identified elements such as C and O with no impurities in CQDs derived from expired passion fruit shells using EDS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DSC of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2I reveal significant variations in the thermal properties of PP-CQDs synthesized at three different temperatures (100, 150, and 200 °C). The sample synthesized at 100 °C shows an exothermic peak at 85.40 °C, with the onset and endset temperatures of 78.23 °C and 110.67 °C, respectively, indicating lower thermal stability and a less crystalline structure due to the presence of loosely bound water molecules. The sample synthesized at 150 °C exhibits a slightly higher exothermic peak at 94.12 °C, with onset and endset temperatures of 89.45 °C and 125.33 °C, suggesting improved thermal stability and crystallinity compared to the 100 °C sample. In contrast, the sample synthesized at 200 °C, which is the selected sample, displays the highest exothermic peak at 103.66 °C, with the onset and endset temperatures of 98.89 °C and 132.85 °C, reflecting the highest thermal stability, crystallinity, and structural integrity among all the samples. This trend indicates that increasing the synthesis temperature enhances the thermal properties of PP-CQDs, with the 200 °C sample showing superior stability and suitability for applications requiring higher thermal resilience. These findings are similar to those of Raul et al., [53], where an exothermic peak between 185 and 339 °C was observed in the DSC plot. This peak can be primarily attributed to the crystallization of silver nanoparticles. Similarly, our analysis of the nanohybrid derived from fruit and vegetable peels revealed a simultaneous occurrence of complete thermal decomposition and crystallization within the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomic force microscopy of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 2J displayed the atomic force microscopy (AFM) which depicts the surface topography of both selected and unselected carbon quantum dot (CQD) samples synthesized under varying temperature conditions. In the selected sample which was synthesized at 200 °C, the AFM analysis reveals a surface with prominent, well-defined peaks and valleys, indicating significant roughness and pronounced topographical features. The height variation is measured to range from −0.4 μm to 3.2 μm, illustrating the considerable structural depth and complexity of the selected sample's surface. This high degree of roughness may be indicative of enhanced crystallinity and graphitization, where higher synthesis temperatures promote the formation of more ordered carbon structures. The two unselected samples synthesized at lower temperatures of 100 and 150 °C exhibit a similar but less pronounced surface topography. Based on comparative analysis, the height variations for these samples are estimated to range approximately between −0.2 μm to 2.5 μm and −0.3 μm to 2.8 μm, respectively. These values suggest that while the unselected samples also possess a rough surface, the extent of roughness was slightly lower compared to the selected sample. This may reflect the incomplete carbonization and less ordered structure in the unselected CQDs. Similar results were reported in a study by Mei et al., [54], where CQDs derived from okra showed a manganese dioxide nanosheet thickness estimated to be approximately 5.2 nm based on their AFM images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 2K present the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the PP-CQDs samples synthesized at 100, 150, and 200 °C temperatures, respectively. All samples exhibit a broad diffraction peak corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating the presence of amorphous carbon structures typical of carbon-based quantum dots. In the two unselected samples which were synthesized at lower temperatures, the XRD patterns show a prominent peak at 2θ ≈ 21–23° for the (002) plane, which is characteristic of the graphitic-like structure but with an increased disorder, suggesting a significant degree of structural randomness and amorphous nature. The broadness of the peak implies that these samples have a lower degree of crystallinity, which is common in CQDs derived from organic precursors through a hydrothermal process. The selected sample, however, shows additional diffraction peaks around 2θ ≈ 33° and 37° corresponding to the (100) and (101) planes, respectively, in addition to the (002) plane. The presence of these additional peaks indicates a higher degree of crystalline order in the selected sample compared to the unselected ones. The (100) and (101) planes are typically associated with the hexagonal graphitic carbon structure, suggesting that this sample may contain graphitic domains with a more organized arrangement of carbon atoms. This suggests that the selection process has effectively enhanced the crystallinity and possibly the graphitic content of the CQDs. This difference in crystallinity could potentially influence the functional properties of the CQDs, such as their photoluminescence and chemical reactivity, making the selected sample more suitable for specific applications where these properties are critical. Similar findings were reported by Manjubaashini et al., [55], who demonstrated similar sharp diffraction peaks in carbon dots synthesized from agro-waste biomass, confirming the presence of graphitic-like structures with high crystallinity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 13C NMR and 1H NMR analysis of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2L displayed the 13C NMR analysis of PP-CQDs samples synthesized at 100, 150, and 200 °C temperatures, respectively to highlight significant differences in their carbon structural environments. The spectra of the unselected samples synthesized at 100 and 150 °C temperatures exhibit numerous peaks primarily in the 60 to 100 ppm region, which corresponds to carbon atoms bonded to electronegative elements such as oxygen or nitrogen, likely indicating the presence of a variety of functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups. The broad and dispersed nature of these peaks suggests a heterogeneous distribution of carbon environments, reflective of the diverse organic residues present in the unselected CQDs. In addition, the 13C NMR spectrum of the selected PP-CQDs sample (synthesized at 200 °C) shows a more defined and concentrated set of peaks in the same region, particularly around 70 to 100 ppm. This indicates a more uniform presence of oxygenated carbon species, such as those found in alcohols, ethers, or carbonyl groups, suggesting that the selection process has resulted in a purer and more consistent sample. The sharper and more pronounced peaks in the selected sample's spectrum reflect a higher degree of structural uniformity, likely due to the removal of a significant portion of the heterogeneous organic material present in the unselected samples.

The 1H NMR analysis of samples of PP-CQDs reveals distinct differences in their chemical composition and structural uniformity as shown in Fig. 2M. The spectra of the unselected samples exhibit multiple peaks in the 3.0 to 5.3 ppm region, indicative of a mixture of hydroxyl (-OH), methylene (-CH₂-), and methine (-CH-) protons. These broad and overlapping peaks suggest the presence of various organic residues and functional groups attached to the carbon dots. Additionally, peaks observed in the 1.0 to 2.0 ppm region are associated with aliphatic protons, likely originating from long-chain hydrocarbons or other organic moieties. In contrast, the selected sample presents a more refined and less crowded 1H NMR spectrum, particularly in the 3.0 to 5.3 ppm region. The peak at around 4.0 ppm in the selected sample is sharper and more defined, suggesting a higher purity of functional groups, possibly due to a reduction in the diversity of organic residues. The aliphatic region (1.0 to 2.0 ppm) in the selected sample also shows more uniform peaks, indicating fewer impurities and a more consistent chemical environment within the CQDs. Similar results were reported by Sharma et al., [56], who conducted the C-NMR and H-NMR of Cissus quadrangularis derived CQDs to investigate the surface functionalities and carbon framework.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quantum yield of PP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the selected PP-CQDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) as a reference material. Solutions of the sample, prepared with deionized water, and the reference material were both set to a concentration of 0.1 μl. Similarly, the reference material was prepared by dissolving quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 to maintain UV–Vis absorbance intensity values at 0.1. Subsequently, fluorescence emission spectra of the concentrated solutions of the PP-CQDs sample and the reference were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The QY of the PP-CQDs was calculated using the standard Eq. (1) and was determined to be 39.15 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence decay time measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence decay time measurements of papaya peel waste-derived carbon quantum dots (PP-CQDs) were successfully conducted using time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. The PP-CQDs exhibited a biexponential decay behavior, indicating the presence of two distinct emissive states. The decay curve was best fitted with a biexponential model, yielding two fluorescence lifetimes: a short lifetime component (τ1) of 3.2 ns and a long lifetime component (τ2) of 9.5 ns.

The short lifetime (τ1) is likely associated with surface defects or functional groups on the carbon dots, which contribute to non-radiative decay processes. The longer lifetime (τ2) is attributed to the intrinsic core states of the carbon dots, which are responsible for the radiative recombination of excitons. The relative amplitudes of these components were found to be A1 = 60 % and A2 = 40 %, indicating a significant contribution from both surface and core states to the overall fluorescence emission of the PP-CQDs. These results are consistent with previous studies of Ai et al., [57] on carbon quantum dots derived from various sources, which also report multiple fluorescence decay components due to different emissive states. The presence of both short and long lifetimes suggests that the PP-CQDs have a heterogeneous structure with multiple emissive sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of a control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: The control and composite hydrogel utilizing carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and pectin embedded without and with CQDs derived from papaya peel waste involves a scientifically rigorous approach that emphasizes the interaction between these components. The synthesis begins with the formulation of hydrogel by dissolving CMC and pectin in aqueous media. CMC, a cellulose derivative modified with carboxymethyl groups, provides soluble characteristics and viscosity, essential for forming a stable gel matrix. Pectin, a naturally occurring polysaccharide, contributes to the network formation due to its rich structural complexity and ability to interact with other polymers. The dissolution processes for both polymers require careful control of temperature and stirring to ensure complete solubilization. The solutions of CMC and pectin are then mixed, and CQDs are added to this polymer base. The incorporation of CQDs is not merely for waste valorization but also enhances the properties of the hydrogel. These CQDs, being nanometric in size, can interact within the polymeric matrix at a molecular level, potentially altering the physical, chemical, and optical properties of the hydrogel. After the homogenous distribution of CQDs throughout the polymer matrix, the gelation process is initiated by adjusting the environmental conditions helpful to network formation. The temperature is another critical factor; higher temperatures can accelerate the mobility of polymer chains and enhance interaction but must be carefully managed to avoid degrading sensitive components like the CQDs. Upon setting these parameters, the hydrogel network begins to form as the polymers and create a three-dimensional matrix that incorporates the CQDs evenly throughout. The final gelation step involves allowing the hydrogel to set, typically at room temperature, to form a stable, uniform gel. This gel is then subjected to a series of washing steps to remove any unincorporated materials or free-floating impurities, ensuring the purity and usability of the final product. This synthesis approach not only demonstrates a practical use of waste-derived materials but also introduces innovative functionalities to traditional hydrogel systems through the strategic integration of nano-enhancements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling and release properties of control and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: This study evaluates the swelling and release properties of control and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite. The control hydrogel exhibited a rapid initial swelling, reaching a maximum swelling ratio (SR) of 10.2 ± 0.5 within 12 h, and then plateaued, indicating the equilibrium state of the hydrogel. The PP-CQDs embedded hydrogel showed a similar initial swelling pattern but reached a slightly higher maximum swelling ratio of 12.7 ± 0.6 within 10 h, before plateauing. The increased swelling ratio of the CQD-embedded hydrogel can be attributed to the presence of PP-CQDs, which may enhance the hydrophilicity and porosity of the hydrogel matrix, allowing for greater water absorption. The concentration of the released drug was measured at the maximum absorbance wavelength (λ max) of 555 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The cumulative release profile of the drug from the control hydrogel showed a steady increase, reaching 70 % of the total drug load within 24 h and plateauing at 80 % by 48 h. The PP-CQDs embedded hydrogel demonstrated a more controlled release, reaching 60 % of the total drug load within 24 h and plateauing at 75 % by 48 h. The release study demonstrated that the PP-CQDs embedded hydrogel exhibited a more controlled and sustained drug release compared to the control hydrogel. This behavior is advantageous for applications in drug delivery, where a prolonged and steady release of therapeutic agents is desired. The slight reduction in the release rate in the presence of CQDs suggests potential interactions between the CQDs and the drug, which may modulate the drug release profile. These findings align with previous studies where the incorporation of nanomaterials into hydrogels has been shown to improve their swelling and release capacity. These results align well with Yu et al. [58] finding observed in the SHNC/PVA/SA/TA-2 hydrogel. This hydrogel demonstrated an impressive equilibrium swelling ratio, exceeding 300 %, indicative of its high capacity to absorb water. Moreover, the release rate of the bioactive substance TA from the hydrogel was found to be over 80 %, further supporting the efficacy of the hydrogel as a delivery system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of a control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
Droplet size and zeta potential analysis of control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique was employed to measure the droplet size and zeta potential of the control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite as shown in Fig. 3A and B. The addition of PP-CQDs into control hydrogel formulations significantly influences their structural and stability characteristics. The average droplet size of the control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite was recorded at 176.4 ± 0.18 and 175.8 ± 0.09 nm, respectively. These droplets can be attributed to the nano-sized dimension of the CQDs and their ability to facilitate a more tightly packed gel matrix, thereby encapsulating smaller droplets within the hydrogel structure. Furthermore, the zeta potential analysis provided insights into the surface charge distribution of the hydrogels, which is essential for understanding their stability in colloidal systems. The control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite exhibited a zeta potential of −14.3 ± 0.22 and −15.8 ± 0.28 mV, demonstrating the inherent stability due to electrostatic repulsion between similarly charged droplets. The more negative zeta potential with PP-CQD addition suggests an increase in surface charge density, likely due to the additional functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, on the surface of the CQDs. These groups can interact with the hydrogel matrix, enhancing electrostatic repulsion forces among the droplets and thereby improving the overall stability of the hydrogel. These findings were similar to Baraz et al., [60], who noted enhanced stability and decreased droplet size of biopolymer hydrogels crosslinked with carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of a PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of PP-CQDs, the control hydrogel, and the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite was assessed to determine cellular response, with results depicted in Fig. 3E. The study aimed to investigate the biocompatibility of all the samples including PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and composite across various concentrations. The PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite showed a promising range of cell viability, with non-significant (p > 0.05) measurements ranging from 94.79±0.25 to 98.13±0.87, 94.82±0.28 to 98.03±0.81 %, and 95.12±0.31 to 98.43±0.96 %, respectively. These results demonstrate the viability of cells exposed to PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and nanohydrogel containing CQDs at concentrations from 1 to 50 mg/mL, indicating a generally low level of cytotoxicity and high cellular compatibility. Significantly (p < 0.05), the hydrogels embedded with the highest concentration of CQDs (50 mg/mL) maintained cell viability nearly comparable to the lower concentrations, underscoring the non-toxic nature of these nano-enhancements. Furthermore, at the lowest concentration range of 0–5 mg/mL, the cytotoxicity results showed a non-significant (p > 0.05) difference in cell viability among the tested concentrations. This suggests that even at minimal incorporation levels, the CQDs do not induce adverse cellular responses, supporting their potential for use in applications requiring direct contact with biological tissues. The cells exposed to PP-CQDs exhibited lower toxicity, likely due to the inert nature of carbon-based materials and the unique surface properties of these quantum dots, which may interact more benignly with cellular structures compared to control hydrogel. Our results correlate well with the observations of Mogharbel et al., [61], who assessed the cytotoxicity of rice straw-derived carbon dots in hydrogels and reported minimal toxicity, thereby validating the safety and applicability of CQD-embedded hydrogels in anticounterfeiting applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: This study evaluated the antimicrobial efficacy of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Fig. 3F presents the zone of inhibition. Notably, the zone of inhibition of control hydrogel was 21.86±0.07, 19.82±0.02, 24.21±0.08, 21.35±0.11, and 20.89±0.05 mm significantly (p < 0.05) smaller than PP-CQDs and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite against E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, S. typhi, and K. pneumoniae (i.e. 24.19±0.06, 22.33±0.02, 27.11±0.02, 24.01±0.12, 23.41±0.13 and 24.95±0.19, 22.93±0.22, 27.24±0.27, 24.38±0.17, 23.94±0.25 mm, respectively). In contrast, the zone of inhibition for positive control (Chloramphenicol) was 29.19±0.15 mm. Furthermore, the MIC and MBC values of the PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite were assessed. The MIC represents the lowest concentration that inhibits visible bacterial growth, while the MBC indicates the lowest concentration that reduces the 99.9 % of the bacterial population. Fig. 2G and H present the MIC and MBC data for each bacterial strain against PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite. The MIC values of control hydrogel were significantly (p < 0.05) different (i.e. 4.11±0.13, 4.39±0.14, 2.43±0.10, 4.78±0.18, 3.92±0.09 μL/mL) from PP-CQDs and nanohydrogel composite for E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, S. typhi, and K. pneumoniae were 6.02±0.01, 6.36±0.07, 3.41±0.13, 6.82±0.19, 5.89±0.06 μL/mL and 6.15±0.12, 6.47±0.06, 3.48±0.21, 6.89±0.24, 5.99±0.17 μL/mL, respectively. The corresponding MBC values for control hydrogel were 2.23±0.03, 2.37±0.08, 1.06±0.07, 2.66±0.01, 2.01±0.04 μL/mL, for PP-CQDs were 3.05±0.11, 3.26±0.12, 1.52±0.09, 3.50±0.1, 2.85±0.24 μL/mL and for nanohydrogel composite were 3.12±0.13, 3.28±0.17, 1.56±0.07, 3.56±0.21, 2.93±0.24 μL/mL respectively. The observed antimicrobial activity of control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite is attributed to two primary mechanisms. First, these nanohydrogels can disrupt vital redox reactions within microbial cells through electron transfer processes, thereby impairing their metabolic functions and ultimately leading to growth inhibition. After that, under light irradiation, hydrogels generate potent reactive oxygen species (ROS) like singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals. These ROS possess inherent antimicrobial properties, causing damage to microbial cell membranes and biomolecules, further contributing to the observed inhibitory effect. On the other hand, hydrogels can exhibit antimicrobial properties without damaging the plant cells of strawberry fruit due to their selective targeting, controlled release, and biocompatibility. The materials, such as CMC and pectin, form a physical barrier that prevents bacterial pathogens from reaching the fruit surface while being non-toxic to the plant cells. In addition, hydrogels have the ability to assist in maintaining moisture and nutritional levels, promote the health of the fruit, and protect plant cells from oxidative stress. This helps to ensure that the antibacterial action does not interfere with the natural cellular processes that occur in the strawberry. The findings are consistent with Luo et al., [62], who demonstrated the significant antimicrobial activity of sulfur and phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots against both E. coli and S. aureus. This study shows that CQDs may be effective against Gram-positive bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofilm activity of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: The PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite showed antibiofilm activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria in Table 1 . The E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, S. typhi, and K. pneumoniae showed significantly (p < 0.05) the lowest antibiofilm activity for control hydrogel 71.65 ± 0.12, 73.36 ± 0.14, 77.28 ± 0.09, 74.76 ± 0.11, 74.03 ± 0.07 %. The effectiveness of the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite can be attributed to its interaction with quorum sensing mechanisms. It regulates the formation of biofilms, which are organized and highly resistant bacterial communities. Additionally, PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite possess unique physicochemical properties that enable them to disrupt quorum-sensing signaling pathways. These nanoscale materials likely interfere with the production and reception of signaling molecules involved in quorum sensing, thereby inhibiting the coordination of bacterial behaviors leading to biofilm formation. A sustainable and environmentally friendly option to prevent bacterial biofilm-related diseases while simultaneously repurposing food waste resources is offered by the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite, which hinders quorum sensing and paves the way for the creation of novel antibiofilm agents. These findings have a similar trend to Sheng et al., [63], who demonstrate the synthesis of antimicrobial carbon dots/pectin-based hydrogel for its high antibiofilm performance, especially in wound healing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time kill kinetic study according to incubation time of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: This study investigated the antimicrobial efficacy of PP-CQDs, control hydrogel, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite against pathogenic microorganisms through a time-kill kinetic analysis as represented in Table 2 . The results revealed significantly higher (p < 0.05) CFU/mL values for PP-CQDs, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite against E. coli (7.73±0.19 and 7.81±0.22 log CFU/mL), S. aureus (7.88±0.03 and 7.96±0.38 log CFU/mL), P. aeruginosa (7.60±0.12 and 7.68±0.30 log CFU/mL), S. typhi (7.66±0.13 and 7.74±0.14 log CFU/mL), and K. pneumoniae (7.53±0.19 and 7.61±0.41 log CFU/mL) than control hydrogel sample, respectively. The potent bactericidal activity of all the samples has been attributed to their ability to induce intracellular reactive oxygen species generation, leading to oxidative stress within bacterial cells. This stress results in damage to proteins, lipids, and DNA, ultimately leading to reduced bacterial viability over time [64]. Beyond ROS generation, samples potentially interfere with vital cellular processes, such as energy metabolism and DNA replication, further contributing to their bactericidal effects. The study found that samples immediately impacted bacterial viability due to their fast antibacterial action. This fast response time makes samples useful for antibacterial applications. The efficient interaction of samples with bacterial cells likely plays a crucial role in their rapid bactericidal effects. This characteristic makes them attractive nanomaterials for time-sensitive applications such as food preservation [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of strawberries coated with formulated hydrogel
Weight loss and pH
ce:section.ce:para: The weight loss and pH values for all strawberry samples including uncoated, coated with control hydrogel, and coated with PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite are presented in Table 3 and Fig. 4A and B. Over the storage period, there was a significant increase (p < 0.05) in weight loss for all fresh-cut strawberries. However, the uncoated strawberries showed a significantly higher weight loss (20.87±0.42 %) compared to the control hydrogel-coated (16.85±0.18 %) and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite-coated samples (16.34±0.39 %). The combination of PP-CQDs and nanohydrogel effectively reduces water vapor permeability, thereby enhancing the moisture barrier of the coated strawberries and minimizing weight loss [66]. These findings are comfirmed to Huo et al., [67], who reported that both control and cellulose paper-coated samples significantly (p < 0.05) impacted weight loss in strawberries. Overall, the study suggests that nanohydrogel coatings are a promising method to reduce weight loss and extend the shelf life of fresh-cut strawberries.

Initially, the pH values for the uncoated, control hydrogel-coated, and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite-coated samples were 3.08 ± 0.037, 3.00 ± 0.021, and 2.98 ± 0.025, respectively, and these values decreased gradually with no significant differences (p > 0.05) throughout the storage period. This decline in pH is likely due to the growth of microorganisms producing organic acids. However, the pH of the uncoated sample (3.77 ± 0.079) had decreased significantly (p < 0.05) compared to the control hydrogel coated (3.52 ± 0.042) and nanohydrogel composite-coated samples (3.48 ± 0.072) on the 10th day of storage. The lower pH in the uncoated strawberries indicated higher microbial and fungal growth. Yu et al., [68] observed similar pH trends in strawberries. Overall, the results demonstrate that the nanohydrogel coating exhibits strong antimicrobial properties, helping to preserve the quality of cut strawberries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TSS
ce:section.ce:para: The TSS content of strawberries coated with control hydrogel (7.63±0.11°B) and PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite (7.58±0.15°B) was significantly lower (p < 0.05) than that of the uncoated strawberries (7.91±0.16°B), as shown in Table 3 and Fig. 4C. This difference is likely due to the PP-CQDs in the nanohydrogel coating, which contributed to the soluble solid content. Throughout storage, TSS levels gradually increased in all samples. This increase is likely due to the breakdown of complex polysaccharides into simpler sugars [69]. However, the TSS of the uncoated strawberries had decreased on the 10th day of storage because sugars were being converted into acids, which is a common sign of fruit spoilage. This trend of decreasing sugar and increasing acidity in decaying fruit aligns with the findings of Sawsan et al., [43].
