Overall, these results suggest that nanohydrogel coatings can enhance the TSS content of fresh-cut strawberries during storage, indicating improved fruit quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Firmness
ce:section.ce:para: During the storage period, all samples of cut strawberries, whether uncoated, coated with control hydrogel, or coated with PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite, indicated a decline in firmness, as illustrated in Table 3 and Fig. 4D. The photo presented in Fig. 4E indicates that the visual quality of strawberries uncoated, coated with control hydrogel, or coated with PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite during the storage. By the 10th day, the uncoated strawberries (1.41 ± 0.17 N) had a notably greater loss of firmness (p > 0.05) compared to the control hydrogel coated (2.22 ± 0.26 N) and nanohydrogel composite-coated samples (2.36 ± 0.15 N). The better retention of firmness in the coated samples can be attributed to the protective barrier formed by the nanohydrogel, which helps to reduce moisture loss and prolongs the shelf life of the cut strawberries. Tabing et al. [70] found that applying an edible aloe vera gel coating to strawberries (Fragaria ananasa L. cv. Sweet Charlie) had similar effects. In summary, the use of coatings helps reduce weight loss and maintain the firmness of cut strawberries over time, compared to uncoated samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Table 4 presents the evaluation of microbial growth on strawberry samples over a period of 10 days. The study compared uncoated strawberries with those coated with a control hydrogel and a PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite. Initially, on the first day of analysis (day 0), no microbial growth was detected in any of the samples. During the storage period, the uncoated and coated strawberries showed no detection of coliforms, yeast, molds, and standard plate counts up until the 4th day of storage. However, on the 10th day of analysis, the strawberries coated with nanohydrogel exhibited a standard plate count of 13 × 104 CFU/g, coliform count of 7 CFU/g, and yeast and mold counts of 11 CFU/g, all of which remained within the acceptable limits recommended by the Food Safety Standard Authority of India (FSSAI) for fresh produce. Conversely, the uncoated strawberries displayed a much higher microbial load by the 4th day of storage, exceeding safe limits, and continued to deteriorate, while the coated strawberries remained within safe microbial limits until the 10th day of storage. These findings align with FSSAI guidelines for fresh fruits, indicating that the application of a nanohydrogel can significantly extend the shelf life of strawberries by effectively controlling microbial growth over the storage period.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensory analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Sensory analysis including appearance, texture, flavor, and overall acceptability of the samples, are presented in Fig. 5 . The uncoated samples received the lowest scores on the 9-point hedonic scale compared to all control hydrogel and formulated hydrogel-coated samples. Among the coated samples, the formulated hydrogel-coated sample received the highest ratings. After 10 days of storage, the uncoated samples had the lowest scores for appearance, likely due to moisture loss from the strawberry fruits. The overall acceptability of the formulated hydrogel-coated strawberry fruits started at 8.5 on day 0 and decreased to 7.5 by the 10th day of analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: This study demonstrates the successful synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from papaya peels via a hydrothermal process, emphasizing a sustainable approach to agricultural waste management. The characterization of PP-CQDs demonstrated their advantageous qualities, including a small size of approximately 4–5 nm, strong fluorescence, exceptional stability, and a broad UV–Vis absorption spectrum. Notably, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) confirmed an average particle size of 0.74 ± 0.04 nm, further emphasizing the potential of this methodology. Theantimicrobial activity of PP-CQDs nanohydrogels against various pathogenic bacteria yielded highly significant (p < 0.05) results. Furthermore, the application of PP-CQDs nanohydrogel composite for coating strawberries showed the promising results in preserving the physicochemical properties of the fruits during storage. The enhanced moisture barrier properties of the PP-CQDs nanohydrogel notably reduced weight loss and maintained firmness and total soluble solids, indicating a valuable application in food preservation. Future studies should concentrate on optimizing the synthesis and functionalization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to improve their effectiveness and examine alternative biowaste materials for CQD production. Furthermore, assessing the environmental impact and biocompatibility of these nanomaterials will be essential for their utilization in several areas, including water purification and environmental remediation. This research illustrates the possibility of generating high-quality fluorescent carbon quantum dots from papaya peels, presenting an achievable approach for sustainable and economical synthesis and food preservation.
